M
Customers AI · Retention
High confidence9 cohorts · YoYLast sync 2m ago
Leading indicator
Repeat rate is up — but new cohorts retain worse
Headline repeat rate rose 4pp YoY, yet M1 retention of new customers fell from 49% to 39%. The loyal base is masking a decline at the front door — fix onboarding before it reaches the headline.
Payback
Payback improved to day 74, from day 98 last year
You monetize faster than you used to — higher margin per order. But the blended 3.4× hides that Paid Meta never pays back. See the channel cut.
Win-back
98 going cold — $95K recoverable
Ranked by historical win-back conversion. The top 20 names hold 60% of the recoverable value — start there, not with a blast.
1 Cohort retention — do new customers come back, and is it getting better or worse?
Retention by acquisition cohort
% of each month’s new customers still ordering · M0 = acquisition month
Cohort
M0
M1
M2
M3
M4
M5
M6
M7
M8
Sep198
100%
49%
42%
37%
33%
30%
28%
26%
25%
Oct212
100%
48%
41%
36%
32%
29%
27%
25%
Nov225
100%
47%
40%
35%
31%
28%
26%
Dec301
100%
45%
38%
33%
29%
26%
Jan243
100%
44%
37%
32%
28%
Feb228
100%
42%
35%
30%
Mar256
100%
41%
34%
Apr271
100%
39%
May186
100%
Month 1 retention↓ -10pp
Sep 49% → Apr 39% · 8 cohorts
Month 3 retention↓ -7pp
Sep 37% → Feb 30% · 6 cohorts
Month 6 retention↓ -2pp
Sep 28% → Nov 26% · 3 cohorts
Compared at equal age only — younger cohorts aren’t measured on months they haven’t reached yet.
Newer cohorts are retaining worse: month-1 retention has slipped from 49% (September) to 39% (April) — a 10-point decline in how well you keep first-time buyers. The loyal base still grows, so the headline looks fine, but the front door is leaking. Something shifted in acquisition or onboarding around Q1.
2 Purchase cycles — when do they reorder, and where does “at risk” begin?
Time between orders
Distribution of days from one order to the next · this is where the segment thresholds come from
4%
20%
34%
18%
10%
8%
6%
0–15
15–30
30–45
45–60
60–75
75–90
90+ days
Median 34 days · healthy
At Risk 51 days · 1.5× cycle
Going Cold 68 days · 2× cycle
The typical customer reorders every 34 days. That rhythm is where the segment thresholds come from — past 51 days they’re At Risk, past 68 they’re Going Cold. Not arbitrary windows; your own measured cadence.
3 LTV & payback — does a customer ever pay back what they cost to acquire?
LTV : CAC
3.4×
↑ from 2.9× last year
Payback
Day 74
24 days faster than last year
This yearLast year
The average customer clears their $420 cost on day 74 — 24 days sooner than a year ago (the faint line). After that, every order is profit, reaching 3.4× CAC over the first year. But a blended number averages winners with losers: switch to Paid Meta.
4 Acquisition × retention — which sources actually build the base, and which just buy one-time orders?
ChannelAcquired3-mo ret.↓Retention qualityLTV:CACVerdict
Email14252%
6.7×Scale
Organic38647%
5.1×Scale
Direct29843%
4.4×Scale
Paid Google41234%
2.8×Hold
Social18828%
2.1×Hold
Paid Meta51419%
0.95×Cut
Email and Organic customers retain more than 2×better than Paid Meta — and cost a fraction to acquire. You’re spending the most to buy the customers who leave fastest. Shift Meta budget toward the channels that retain.
Coupon-acquired · 392 customers
Repeat rate
18%
Avg LTV
$1,200
LTV:CAC
1.4×
Full-price acquired · 1,748 customers
Repeat rate
44%
Avg LTV
$3,100
LTV:CAC
4.2×
A first-order discount buys a customer who doesn’t come back: coupon-acquired customers repeat at 18% versus 44% at full price, returning under a third of the lifetime value. You paid them to leave.
5 Going cold — who’s slipping away, and what’s worth recovering?
8 going cold · $185K exposed · $95K recoverable— ranked by win-back value, not by who’s loudest

$640
recoverable

The top 20 names hold roughly 60% of the recoverable value. A targeted sequence to those — not a blast to all 98 — is how win-back stays profitable. Send the ranked list to the Bridge to build the flow.

Retention is where a customer business is won or lost: you’re either buying one-time orders, or building customers who pay back what they cost and then some. This room answers do they come back, does it pay off, which acquisition builds the base, and are we getting better or worse.

Ask Magellan anything…
